text.title: Roadmap Audience Segmentation
text.summary: Stakeholder communication matrix showing what each audience needs from a roadmap artifact and why the same document cannot serve all audiences.

rule.statement: Create separate roadmap artifacts for each audience from one source. Never share the same document with all stakeholders.
rule.rationale: Engineering needs sprint-level detail and dependencies. Customers need capability descriptions without implementation specifics. The board needs outcome metrics with quarterly targets. Sales needs problem-solution framing with a confidence signal. One document that serves all serves none.

rule.statement: External / customer-facing roadmaps: use theme-based abstractions, no specific dates — use "H1 2026" or "Coming Soon" horizon labels instead.
rule.rationale: Specific dates become contractual commitments in customers' minds. Abstracted horizons communicate direction without creating support escalations when dates slip.

rule.statement: Board / executive roadmaps: lead with outcome metrics and quarterly targets. Include confidence levels. Do not include feature lists — executives need to know if the product will hit the business goal, not which features ship in Q2.
rule.rationale: Boards are accountable for business outcomes. A feature list answer to a business goal question signals misalignment between product and strategy.

rule.statement: Engineering roadmaps: include outcome targets, confidence levels, sprint-level detail for Now, and options (not THE solution) for Next. Dependencies and blockers must be explicit.
rule.rationale: Engineers who understand the outcome can propose better solutions. Options framing ("we have three approaches to this outcome") produces better technical decisions than feature mandates.

rule.statement: Sales roadmaps: lead with the problem being solved, then the solution. Include a confidence signal ("confident", "exploring"). Only include items you can commit to when deals close within 90 days.
rule.rationale: Sales uses roadmaps to close deals. Uncommitted items that end up on sales decks create product commitments the team cannot honor.
